museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A holding month with the market fully awake: Spectrum owners learn patience, arcade hits keep earning, and the ColecoVision's arcade-quality promise moves closer.
museum.curatorNote: June is mostly contextual. It sits between the Spectrum launch and the August hardware wave, so the exhibit avoids false exact releases and focuses on safe market movement.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: The boom widens rather than pivots.

items.title: Spectrum ownership becomes a routine
items.body: Early adopters moved from curiosity to use: loading tapes, typing listings and discovering what 16K or 48K really meant.

items.title: ColecoVision's promise grows louder
items.body: Before national rollout, Coleco's pitch was already clear: bring arcade-quality Donkey Kong home.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision launched around late July/August with Donkey Kong pack-in.

items.title: Arcades remain the premium tier
items.body: Ms. Pac-Man, Dig Dug, Zaxxon and Robotron showed how wide the arcade design palette had become.

items.title: Third-party cartridge makers gain visibility
items.body: Activision and Imagic were part of a home-console shelf no longer controlled by Atari alone.
items.sourceNote: Pitfall and Demon Attack later 1982 context; third-party publishers active before then.

items.title: UK game buying becomes fragmented
items.body: Arcades, console cartridges, microcomputer tapes and magazine listings all counted as games, but they did not feel like one market yet.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: June's shelf is a living catalogue more than a launch list.

items.title: Spectrum early games
items.body: Small cassette titles and listings began building the platform's identity.
items.sourceNote: Specific June title dates not asserted.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: Still fresh internationally and easy to explain across a crowded arcade.

items.title: Robotron: 2084
items.body: Becoming a shorthand for impossible arcade intensity.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: The sequel that looked less like a sequel and more like the better-known version for many players.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Still the year's depth illusion showpiece.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: Mass-market home ownership and mass-market criticism continued side by side.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: Already a hit, now becoming the future pack-in that would sell a console.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision launched with Donkey Kong pack-in later in 1982.

items.title: C64 software promise
items.body: The C64 was not yet a normal retail gaming object, but its hardware made games feel inevitable.
items.sourceNote: C64 introduced in January, marketed from August.

items.title: Spectrum memory tiers
items.body: 16K and 48K were not abstract numbers; they shaped what games could be and what owners wished they had bought.

items.title: ColecoVision anticipation
items.body: The pitch was simple: arcade conversions that looked closer to cabinets than the Atari 2600 could manage.

items.title: Atari 2600 pressure
items.body: The 2600 still dominated, but new consoles and third-party publishers made dominance feel less secure.

items.title: Arcade cabinet economy
items.body: Cabinets were still location-based events, maintained and moved by operators.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: June magazines made the market feel bigger than any one machine.

items.title: C&VG summer shelf
items.headline: Arcades, micros and home consoles together
items.curatorNote: The title itself captured the split market: computer and video games, not yet one settled category.
items.body: The title itself captured the split market: computer and video games, not yet one settled category.

items.title: Electronic Games market confidence
items.headline: The boom in review form
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for June 1982.
items.curatorNote: The American press still had the bright certainty of an expanding medium.
items.body: The American press still had the bright certainty of an expanding medium.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly listings
items.headline: Weekly code and adverts
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for June 1982.
items.curatorNote: Weekly print made the home-computer world feel unusually immediate.
items.body: Weekly print made the home-computer world feel unusually immediate.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Machines as investments
items.curatorNote: Buying a computer was still sold as serious; games slipped in through the side door.
items.body: Buying a computer was still sold as serious; games slipped in through the side door.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The summer network was paper, cassette and word of mouth.

items.title: Weekly print worked like a feed
items.body: The delay between issues mattered less when nothing else moved faster for most players.

items.title: Software arrived by post
items.body: Mail order turned adverts into slow downloads.

items.title: Arcades were local networks
items.body: A cabinet's reputation spread across schools and streets.

items.title: Modems stayed invisible
items.body: Networked computing was real but not yet ordinary gaming life.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: June felt like waiting for the next machine while learning the one you had.

items.title: The Spectrum was still being understood
items.body: Owners were learning what memory, loading and BASIC meant in practice.

items.title: Arcades kept their glamour
items.body: Home systems were improving, but the cabinet still looked expensive and alive.

items.title: The console shelf was about to change
items.body: ColecoVision's promise made Atari's dominance feel less permanent.

items.title: Choice felt exciting, not exhausting
items.body: Every machine seemed to open a different door.
